made some changes in response to @PieterjanRobbe's comments over at https://github.com/JuliaGeometry/DelaunayTriangulation.jl/issues/181, and made some other minor changes. To summarise: 1. Since I just finished implementing weighted triangulations and power diagrams this weekend, I've mentioned them in the summary as a feature that the package supports. 2. I changed the language of "The most feature-rich" in comparing my package to other software. I now just claim to support features that most of the other packages don't support, which I think should be appropriate. I also now mention that there are other Julia packages that support triangulations, and link to where I discuss that in my package's README since it would be a bit cluttered to also do a full comparison in the paper. 3. The summary paragraphs have been reordered. I now mention the package first and then, in the second paragraph, define triangulations and tessellations. 4. Since random power diagrams often look bad, I changed the README example to use a `rng` object for reproducibility. I also made the example use a bit less text width. Thanks again to @PieterjanRobbe for the comments, I appreciate it.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/openjournals/joss-reviews/issues/comments/2340398780/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

> 
> ```
> julia> LinearAlgebra.promote_leaf_eltypes(Any[123.4])
> Float64
> 
> julia> LinearAlgebra.promote_leaf_eltypes(Number[123.4])
> Number
> ```
> 
> That should return `Float64` in both cases. As a consequence, `isapprox` would and should return `true` in all cases.

The first two methods in

```julia
promote_leaf_eltypes(x::Union{AbstractArray{T},Tuple{T,Vararg{T}}}) where {T<:Number} = T
promote_leaf_eltypes(x::Union{AbstractArray{T},Tuple{T,Vararg{T}}}) where {T<:NumberArray} = eltype(T)
promote_leaf_eltypes(x::T) where {T} = T
promote_leaf_eltypes(x::Union{AbstractArray,Tuple}) = mapreduce(promote_leaf_eltypes, promote_type, x; init=Bool)
```

are probably not ideal when `T` is not a concrete type, since `T <: Number` for e.g. `Number`, `Real`, `Complex`, and `AbstractFloat`. In these cases they don't match the definition in the last line, or the documented behaviour of this function being the same as `promote_type(typeof(leaf1), typeof(leaf2), ...)`. Perhaps these methods need to be updated to account for this case.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2267093395/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

```julia-repl
(@v1.10) pkg> up nomatchname
    Updating registry at `C:\Users\danjv\.julia\registries\General.toml`
ERROR: The following package names could not be resolved:
 * nomatchname (not found in project or manifest)

julia> versioninfo()
Julia Version 1.10.4
Commit 48d4fd4843 (2024-06-04 10:41 UTC)
Build Info:
  Official https://julialang.org/ release
Platform Info:
  OS: Windows (x86_64-w64-mingw32)
  CPU: 12 × 12th Gen Intel(R) Core(TM) i5-1235U
  WORD_SIZE: 64
  LIBM: libopenlibm
  LLVM: libLLVM-15.0.7 (ORCJIT, alderlake)
Threads: 1 default, 0 interactive, 1 GC (on 12 virtual cores)
```

On v1.11-rc1, the suggestions are now shown even if there are none to show:

```julia-repl
(notes) pkg> up nomatchname
    Updating registry at `C:\Users\danjv\.julia\registries\General.toml`
ERROR: The following package names could not be resolved:
 * nomatchname (not found in project or manifest)
   Suggestions:

julia> versioninfo()
Julia Version 1.11.0-rc1
Commit 3a35aec36d (2024-06-25 10:23 UTC)
Build Info:
  Official https://julialang.org/ release
Platform Info:
  OS: Windows (x86_64-w64-mingw32)
  CPU: 12 × 12th Gen Intel(R) Core(TM) i5-1235U
  WORD_SIZE: 64
  LLVM: libLLVM-16.0.6 (ORCJIT, alderlake)
Threads: 8 default, 0 interactive, 4 GC (on 12 virtual cores)
Environment:
  JULIA_EDITOR = code
  JULIA_NUM_THREADS = 8
```

Not sure why it's happening now since it looks like this case is still being checked for in https://github.com/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/blob/e6880bc9d8a04d95df6e341c76786219a4efc33f/src/Types.jl#L1113","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/3958/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/Pkg.jl/issues/3958/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

In doing so I am having some difficulties dealing with iteration and indexing, and the error messages don't really seem so clear to me in how I can fix it.

The following code covers the type of iteration I am dealing with:
```
using DifferentialEquations, ModelingToolkit, Symbolics
@inline do_something(k) = sin(k^2)
@register_symbolic do_something(k) 
@inline lookup_val(dict, idx) = dict[idx] # getindex
@register_symbolic lookup_val(dict, idx)
@inline set_val!(dict, val, idx) = dict[idx] = val # setindex!
@register_symbolic set_val!(dict, val, idx)
function ode_f_sym(du, u, p, t)
    vals, u_idx, itr_idx = p 
    for (i, j) in itr_idx
        set_val!(du, lookup_val(vals, i) * lookup_val(u, j), i)
    end
    for k in u_idx 
        set_val!(du, lookup_val(du, k) + do_something(lookup_val(u, k)), k)
    end
    nothing 
end 
a, b, c, d, e = 1.7, 2.3, -2.0, 10.0, 7.19
u_idx = [2, 3, 5, 4, 1] 
itr_idx = Dict(1 => 3, 2 => 5, 3 => 1, 4 => 4, 5 => 2)
prob = ODEProblem(ode_f_sym, [0.0, 1.0, 2.3, -2.0, 1.0], (0.0, 1.0), ((a, b, c, d, e), u_idx, itr_idx))
modelingtoolkitize(prob)
```
I define additional functions for `getindex` and `setindex!` so that they are not traced (as someone helped me with here https://discourse.julialang.org/t/modelingtoolkit-indexing-a-parameter-by-another-parameter-in-an-odeproblem/83569/2?u=legola18), along with some additional `do_something` function. When I run `modelingtoolkitize` as shown, I get:
```
ERROR: BoundsError: attempt to access Num at index [2]
Stacktrace:

How can I change the code further to allow for this type of work? It would be nice if there were e.g. a suggestion in this error message for what could be done for this type of loop, too - I know improving these error messages is a current work in progress.

Just for reference, this is what the code above looks like with hard-coded numbers (to make it easier to compare to for what 'works') and the Jacobian that I actually want to compute:
```
@inline do_something(k) = sin(k^2)
function ode_f_basic(du, u, p, t)
    vals= p 
    du[1] = vals[1] * u[3] 
    du[2] = vals[2] * u[5] 
    du[3] = vals[3] * u[1] 
    du[4] = vals[4] * u[4]

    du[3] += do_something(u[3])
    du[5] += do_something(u[5])
    nothing 
end 
a, b, c, d, e = 1.7, 2.3, -2.0, 10.0, 7.19
u_idx = [2, 3, 5]

de = modelingtoolkitize(prob)
calculate_jacobian(de)

docs https://docs.sciml.ai/Optimization/stable/API/optimization_function/ mention that the provided forms for `grad`, `hess`, `hv`, `cons`, `cons_j`, `cons_h`, and `lag_h` can be provided as an in-place form e.g. `cons(res, x, p)`, or an out-of-place form e.g. `cons(x, p)`. This doesn't seem to be the case, e.g.

```julia
using Optimization
using OptimizationOptimJL
rosenbrock(x, p) = (p[1] - x[1])^2 + p[2] * (x[2] - x[1]^2)^2
x0 = zeros(2)
_p = [1.0, 100.0]
cons = (x, p) -> [x[1]^2 + x[2]^2]
optf = OptimizationFunction(rosenbrock, Optimization.AutoForwardDiff(); cons = cons)
prob = OptimizationProblem(optf, x0, _p, lcons = [-5.0], ucons = [10.0])
sol = solve(prob, IPNewton()) 
```

Looking at the `instantiate_function` code, it doesn't seem like this is meant to be supported, e.g. here in https://github.com/SciML/Optimization.jl/blob/master/src/function/finitediff.jl:

https://github.com/SciML/Optimization.jl/blob/master/src/function/finitediff.jl#L87-L91

No check is made for this. I don't think it would be too hard to correct this, e.g. the code above could, I think, be something like

```julia
if f.cons === nothing 
    cons = nothing 
else 
    if SciMLBase.isinplace(f.cons, 3)
        cons = (res, θ) -> f.cons(res, θ, p)
    else 
        cons = (res, θ) -> cons .= f.cons(θ, p)
    end
end
```

Similar problems e.g. in https://github.com/SciML/Optimization.jl/blob/master/src/function/forwarddiff.jl.

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/NonlinearSolve.jl/issues/204","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/NonlinearSolve.jl","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/NonlinearSolve.jl/issues/204/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/NonlinearSolve.jl/issues/204/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/NonlinearSolve.jl/issues/204/events","html_url":"https://github.com/SciML/NonlinearSolve.jl/issues/204","id":1887560838,"node_id":"I_kwDOEV8z8M5wgeSG","number":204,"title":"(SteadyState/Nonlinear)Problems from ODEProblems ignore kwargs","user":{"login":"DanielVandH","id":95613936,"node_id":"U_kgDOBbLz8A","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/95613936?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH","html_url":"https://github.com/DanielVandH","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2023-09-08T12:11:41Z","updated_at":"2023-09-08T12:11:41Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"When a `SteadyStateProblem` or a `NonlinearProblem` is created from an `ODEProblem`, the `ODEProblem`'s keyword arguments don't get passed. For example, callbacks don't get passed:

```julia
using NonlinearSolve, SteadyStateDiffEq, OrdinaryDiffEq
f = (u, p, t) -> u - u^2 
cond = (u, t, integrator) -> true
aff = integrator -> @show integrator.t
cb = DiscreteCallback(cond, aff)
prob = ODEProblem(f, 1/2, (0.0, 1.0), callback=cb)
sprob = SteadyStateProblem(prob)
sol = solve(sprob, DynamicSS(Tsit5()))
```

nothing gets `@show`n since `sprob`'s underlying `ODEProblem` didn't get any kwargs. The constructor 

```julia
function SteadyStateProblem(prob::AbstractODEProblem)
    SteadyStateProblem{isinplace(prob)}(prob.f, prob.u0, prob.p)
end
```

needs to be (I think)

```julia
function SteadyStateProblem(prob::AbstractODEProblem)
    SteadyStateProblem{isinplace(prob)}(prob.f, prob.u0, prob.p; prob.kwargs...)
end
```

and similarly for `NonlinearProblem`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/NonlinearSolve.jl/issues/204/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/NonlinearSolve.jl/issues/204/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

```

If just using normal functions:

```julia

 f1 = (du, u, p, t) -> u[1]
 f2 = (du, u, p, t) -> u[2] 
 f = SplitFunction(f1, f2, jac_prototype = sparse([1.0 1.0; 1.0 1.0]))
 prob = SplitODEProblem(f, [1.0, 1.0], (0.0, 1.0))
 solve(prob, TRBDF2(linsolve=KLUFactorization()), saveat=0.1)
```

```julia-repl
julia> f.jac_prototype
2×2 SparseMatrixCSC{Float64, Int64} with 4 stored entries:
 1.0  1.0
 1.0  1.0

julia> prob.f.jac_prototype

julia> prob.f.f1.jac_prototype

```

because `OptimJLOptimizationCache` has

```julia
!(opt isa Optim.ZerothOrderOptimizer) && f.grad === nothing &&
        error("Use OptimizationFunction to pass the derivatives or automatically generate them with one of the autodiff backends")
```

but, with constraints, `opt` in this case is 

```julia
opt = Fminbox{NelderMead{Optim.AffineSimplexer, Optim.AdaptiveParameters}, Float64, Optim.var"#49#51"}(NelderMead{Optim.AffineSimplexer, Optim.AdaptiveParameters}(Optim.AffineSimplexer(0.025, 0.5), Optim.AdaptiveParameters(1.0, 1.0, 0.75, 1.0)), NaN, 0.001, Optim.var"#49#51"())
```

I think something like 

```julia
function _optim_requires_grad(opt)
    if opt isa Optim.ConstrainedOptimizer 
        return true 
    elseif opt isa Optim.AbstractConstrainedOptimizer # Optim.ConstrainedOptimizer isn't sufficient - Fminbox isn't one
        return _optim_requires_grad(opt.method)
    else
        return !(opt isa Optim.ZerothOrderOptimizer)
    end
end
```

is needed, e.g.

```julia
_optim_requires_grad(NelderMead()) # false 
_optim_requires_grad(Fminbox(NelderMead())) # false
_optim_requires_grad(LBFGS()) # true
_optim_requires_grad(Fminbox(LBFGS())) # true
_optim_requires_grad(IPNewton()) # true 
```

Haven't tested anything for any of the other packages.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Optimization.jl/issues/548/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Optimization.jl/issues/548/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

using NonlinearSolve, SciMLNLSolve

u0 = [1.0, 1.0]
p = 2.0 
probN = NonlinearProblem(f, u0, p)
solver = solve(probN, NLSolveJL(method = :fixedpoint))
```

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/LinearSolve.jl/issues/302","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/LinearSolve.jl","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/LinearSolve.jl/issues/302/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/LinearSolve.jl/issues/302/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/LinearSolve.jl/issues/302/events","html_url":"https://github.com/SciML/LinearSolve.jl/issues/302","id":1708474871,"node_id":"I_kwDOFsu4xM5l1UH3","number":302,"title":"solving the same problem twice is broken","user":{"login":"karajan9","id":6549509,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY1NDk1MDk=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/6549509?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/karajan9","html_url":"https://github.com/karajan9","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/karajan9/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/karajan9/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/karajan9/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/karajan9/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/karajan9/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/karajan9/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/karajan9/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/karajan9/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/karajan9/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2023-05-13T06:57:04Z","updated_at":"2023-05-13T15:41:17Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"On Julia 1.9.0 with LinearSolve 1.42.0 solving the same problem twice doesn't give the same result:
```
A = [1.0 2.0; 3.0 4.0]
b = [1.0, 2.0]
prob = LinearProblem(A, b)
linsolve = init(prob)
sol1 = solve(linsolve)
# julia> sol1.u
# 2-element Vector{Float64}:
#  0.0
#  0.5
sol2 = solve(linsolve)
# julia> sol2.u
# 2-element Vector{Float64}:
#  -10.999999999999995
#    8.499999999999996
```
This is also true when `set_b` is used in between.
```
A = [1.0 2.0; 3.0 4.0]
b = [1.0, 2.0]
prob = LinearProblem(A, b)
linsolve = init(prob)
sol1 = solve(linsolve)
# julia> sol1.u
# 2-element Vector{Float64}:
#  0.0
#  0.5
linsolve = LinearSolve.set_b(linsolve, [2.0, 3.0])
sol2 = solve(linsolve)
# julia> sol2.u
# 2-element Vector{Float64}:
# -15.999999999999993
#  12.499999999999995
# should be [-1.0, 1.5]
```

That means that the examples in the README are currently broken.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/LinearSolve.jl/issues/302/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/LinearSolve.jl/issues/302/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/LinearSolve.jl/issues/comments/1546694017","html_url":"https://github.com/SciML/LinearSolve.jl/issues/302#issuecomment-1546694017","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/LinearSolve.jl/issues/302","id":1546694017,"node_id":"IC_kwDOFsu4xM5cMK2B","user":{"login":"DanielVandH","id":95613936,"node_id":"U_kgDOBbLz8A","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/95613936?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH","html_url":"https://github.com/DanielVandH","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-05-13T15:41:17Z","updated_at":"2023-05-13T15:41:17Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"Could be related to the cache being still treated as fresh after solving:

Indeed those tests should definitely include some random testing.

That first point is because `set_A` just does `@set! cache.A = A`, in contrast to the initial use of `init` which has an `alias_A` keyword argument to allow `deepcopy` to be used by default - the `set_A` and `set_b` functions need `alias_A = default_alias_A(cache.alg, cache.A, cache.b)` and `alias_b = ...` keyword arguments, just like `init` has.

```
A = [1.0 2.0; 3.0 4.0]
b = [1.0, 2.0]
prob = LinearProblem(A, b)
linsolve = init(prob)
sol1 = solve(linsolve)
# julia> sol1.u
# 2-element Vector{Float64}:
#  0.0
#  0.5
sol2 = solve(linsolve)
# julia> sol2.u
# 2-element Vector{Float64}:
#  -10.999999999999995
#    8.499999999999996
```
This is also true when `set_b` is used in between.
```
A = [1.0 2.0; 3.0 4.0]
b = [1.0, 2.0]
prob = LinearProblem(A, b)
linsolve = init(prob)
sol1 = solve(linsolve)
# julia> sol1.u
# 2-element Vector{Float64}:
#  0.0
#  0.5
linsolve = LinearSolve.set_b(linsolve, [2.0, 3.0])
sol2 = solve(linsolve)
# julia> sol2.u
# 2-element Vector{Float64}:
# -15.999999999999993
#  12.499999999999995
# should be [-1.0, 1.5]
```

That means that the examples in the README are currently docs https://docs.sciml.ai/Optimization/stable/API/optimization_function/ mention that the provided forms for `grad`, `hess`, `hv`, `cons`, `cons_j`, `cons_h`, and `lag_h` can be provided as an in-place form e.g. `cons(res, x, p)`, or an out-of-place form e.g. `cons(x, p)`. This doesn't seem to be the case, e.g.

```julia
using Optimization
using OptimizationOptimJL
rosenbrock(x, p) = (p[1] - x[1])^2 + p[2] * (x[2] - x[1]^2)^2
x0 = zeros(2)
_p = [1.0, 100.0]
cons = (x, p) -> [x[1]^2 + x[2]^2]
optf = OptimizationFunction(rosenbrock, Optimization.AutoForwardDiff(); cons = cons)
prob = OptimizationProblem(optf, x0, _p, lcons = [-5.0], ucons = [10.0])
sol = solve(prob, IPNewton()) 
```

Looking at the `instantiate_function` code, it doesn't seem like this is meant to be supported, e.g. here in https://github.com/SciML/Optimization.jl/blob/master/src/function/finitediff.jl:

https://github.com/SciML/Optimization.jl/blob/master/src/function/finitediff.jl#L87-L91

No check is made for this. I don't think it would be too hard to correct this, e.g. the code above could, I think, be something like

```julia
if f.cons === nothing 
    cons = nothing 
else 
    if SciMLBase.isinplace(f.cons, 3)
        cons = (res, θ) -> f.cons(res, θ, p)
    else 
        cons = (res, θ) -> cons .= f.cons(θ, p)
    end
end
```

Similar problems e.g. in https://github.com/SciML/Optimization.jl/blob/master/src/function/forwarddiff.jl.

What might be needed to extend EnsembleProblem from DifferentialEquations.jl to OptimizationProblems? 

I provide an example of the type of thing I would want to do with it, re-running solve on an OptimizationProblem many times with new `p` each time, used for running a simulation study on some regression coefficients. 

```julia
using Optimization, OptimizationNLopt, ElasticArrays, StatsBase

## Define EnsembleProblem
struct EnsembleOptimizationProblem{P,O,PF,R,U}
    prob::P
    output_func::O # (sol, i)
    prob_func::PF # (prob, i) (ignoring the repeat argument for this issue)
    reduction_func::R # (u, data) (ignoring the I argument for this issue)
    simulations::Int64
    u_init::U # initial allocator
end
struct EnsembleOptimizationSolution{S}
    sols::S
end
function __solve(ensemble_prob, optim_alg)
    num_simulations = ensemble_prob.simulations
    initial_prob = ensemble_prob.prob
    u_init = ensemble_prob.u_init
    for i in 1:num_simulations
        new_prob = ensemble_prob.prob_func(initial_prob, i) # safetycopy should be an option for deepcopy here
        sol = solve(new_prob, optim_alg)
        output, rerun = ensemble_prob.output_func(sol, i) # ignoring rerun for this issue
        u_init, converged = ensemble_prob.reduction_func(u_init, output)
    end
    return EnsembleOptimizationSolution(u_init)
end

## The objective function
function objective_function(θ::AbstractVector{T}, p) where {T}
    β₀, β₁, σ = θ
    (; group, growth) = p
    n = length(group)
    err = zero(T)
    for (x, y) in zip(group, growth)
        ŷ = β₀ + β₁ * x
        err += (y - ŷ)^2
    end
    err = -0.5n * log(2π * σ^2) - inv(2σ^2) * err
    return -err
end

## Define the simulation study
ngroup = 2
nrep = 10
β₀ = 5
β₁ = -2.0
σ = 2.0
group = repeat([false, true], inner=nrep)
ε = σ * randn(ngroup * nrep)
growth = @. β₀ + β₁ * group + ε
p = (group=group, growth=growth)
opt_prob = OptimizationProblem(objective_function, rand(3), p)
output_func = (sol, i) -> (sol.u, false) # don't care about the objective
prob_func = (prob, i) -> begin # get new growths
    new_growth = β₀ .+ β₁ .* group .+ σ * randn(ngroup * nrep)
    return remake(
        prob,
        p=(group=group, growth=new_growth)
    )
end
reduction_func = (u, batch) -> (append!(u, batch), false)

## Solve the EnsembleProblem
ens_prob = EnsembleOptimizationProblem(opt_prob, output_func, prob_func, reduction_func, 500, ElasticMatrix{Float64}(undef, 3, 0))
ens_sol = __solve(ens_prob, NLopt.LN_NELDERMEAD)

## Look at e.g. how well we capture the true regression coefficients
mat = ens_sol.sols
mean(mat; dims=2)
cint = x -> (quantile(x, 0.025), quantile(x, 0.975))
[cint(θ) for θ in eachrow(mat)]
```

```
using NonlinearSolve

function mwe(out, u, p)
    out[1] = u[1] - p[1]
end

p = [5.0]

prob = NonlinearProblem(mwe, [5.2], p=p)
u = solve(prob, NewtonRaphson())

prob2 = remake(prob, p=[6.0])
u2 = solve(prob2, NewtonRaphson())
```

Results in:
```
julia> u
u: 1-element Vector{Float64}:
 5.0

julia> u2
u: 1-element Vector{Float64}:
 5.0
```

But when looking at `?remake`:

```
 remake(prob::NonlinearProblem; f = missing, u0 = missing, p = missing,
      problem_type = missing, kwargs = missing, _kwargs...)

  Remake the given NonlinearProblem. If u0 or p are given as symbolic maps ModelingToolkit.jl has to be loaded.
```

I don't know if this is expected behavior (and maybe should result in an error or a warning) or should just be fixed, but I felt like it should be reported as it can result in a silent wrong problem is that `p` is not a keyword argument for `NonlinearProblem`, so passing `p` as you are doing it gets pushed into `prob.kwargs` instead:

```julia
julia> prob = NonlinearProblem(mwe, [5.2], p=p)
NonlinearProblem with uType Vector{Float64}. In-place: true
u0: 1-element Vector{Float64}:
 5.2

julia> prob.p
SciMLBase.NullParameters()

julia> prob.kwargs
pairs(::NamedTuple) with 1 entry:
  :p => [5.0]
```
It works if you don't do this -

```julia
using NonlinearSolve

function mwe(out, u, p)
    @show p
    out[1] = u[1] - p[1]
end

p = [5.0]

prob = NonlinearProblem(mwe, [5.2], p)
prob.p 
prob.kwargs 

u = solve(prob, NewtonRaphson())

prob2 = remake(prob, p=[6.0])
u2 = solve(prob2, NewtonRaphson())
```
```julia
julia> u
u: 1-element Vector{Float64}:
 5.0

julia> u2
u: 1-element Vector{Float64}:
 6.0

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/PDEBase.jl/issues/8","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/PDEBase.jl","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/PDEBase.jl/issues/8/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/PDEBase.jl/issues/8/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/PDEBase.jl/issues/8/events","html_url":"https://github.com/SciML/PDEBase.jl/issues/8","id":1648379550,"node_id":"I_kwDOJM37lM5iQEae","number":8,"title":"A request for comment - Symbolic PDE Frontend","user":{"login":"xtalax","id":9698054,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjk2OTgwNTQ=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/9698054?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/xtalax","html_url":"https://github.com/xtalax","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/xtalax/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/xtalax/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/xtalax/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/xtalax/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/xtalax/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/xtalax/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/xtalax/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/xtalax/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/xtalax/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2023-03-30T21:56:33Z","updated_at":"2023-03-30T23:09:36Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Calling all lurkers, yes you 👀.

Chances are if you are reading this you are interested in solving PDEs - we want to hear your ideas for a common base either here or in an issue.

What information do you need to discretize a system of PDEs, mathematically defined with ModelingToolkit.jl, in your package of choice, returning a SciMLSystem with `symbolic_discretize`, or SciMLProblem with `discretize`, or both?

We have a system for recognizing location and type of boundary condition, and extracting independent and dependent variables from the equations. In MethodOfLines.jl and (soon) NeuralPDE.jl we are using Symbolics and SymbolicUtils as an IR, see [here](https://symbolicutils.juliasymbolics.org/rewrite/) for information on how to use rewrite rules, and [here](https://docs.sciml.ai/MethodOfLines/stable/devnotes/) for how a simple discretizer is implemented in MOL. We want to expand this common parsing to get enough information to make it easy to discretize symbolic systems in the package of your choice.

We are hoping to collect a case study of how to write a discretizer to include in the docs for others to learn. If we can align on a common frontend language and parser (optional in stages), and common benchmarking based on [PDESystemLibrary](https://github.com/SciML/PDESystemLibrary.jl), we can massively improve the state of solving PDEs in Julia.

But what IR/form do you need for the main equations? What about [domain types](https://github.com/SciML/PDEBase.jl/issues/5)? What is important to you? Any ideas please let us know. 

Any questions about the current state of the SciML PDEs ecosystem also welcome.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/PDEBase.jl/issues/8/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/PDEBase.jl/issues/8/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/PDEBase.jl/issues/comments/1491080422","html_url":"https://github.com/SciML/PDEBase.jl/issues/8#issuecomment-1491080422","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/PDEBase.jl/issues/8","id":1491080422,"node_id":"IC_kwDOJM37lM5Y4BTm","user":{"login":"DanielVandH","id":95613936,"node_id":"U_kgDOBbLz8A","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/95613936?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH","html_url":"https://github.com/DanielVandH","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2023-03-30T23:09:36Z","updated_at":"2023-03-30T23:09:36Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"Replying to [FiniteVolumeMethod](https://github.com/DanielVandH/FiniteVolumeMethod.jl/issues/29). I discuss a lot of this at https://danielvandh.github.io/FiniteVolumeMethod.jl/stable/interface/ and https://danielvandh.github.io/FiniteVolumeMethod.jl/stable/math/. 

A high-level overview is as follows:
- The geometry is represented with a Delaunay triangulation, allowing for efficient iteration over mesh elements (triangles). The domains supported here are meant to be pretty generic. The triangulation is implemented in my other package https://github.com/DanielVandH/DelaunayTriangulation.jl. Currently this package is primarily used for my [Gmsh interface](https://danielvandh.github.io/DelaunayTriangulation.jl/stable/triangulations/gmsh/) which requires only a boundary to be specified, although I'm working on implementing constrained triangulations, (adaptive) mesh refinement, and advancing front methods so that there is a better Julia interface for this without needing Gmsh.
- The boundary by a collection of boundary curves that are themselves represented as ordered polygonal chains. This way, each part of the boundary is identified by a tuple `(i, j)`, where `i` is the boundary curve (`i = 1` for the outermost curve, `i > 1` for inner curves) and `j` is the `j`th chain of this curve. With this setup, it is easy to associate boundary conditions to each `(i, j)`, and each individual segment can be used for computing normal vectors, etc. 
- The supported boundary conditions are Dirichlet, Neumann, and `du/dt = ` types, which are provided for each `(i, j)`. You just provide a list of functions and a corresponding list of boundary condition types, e.g. `(f, g, h)` and `(:D, :N, :dudt)` would associate with the curves `1`, `2`, and `3` the functions `f`, `g`, and `h`, respectively, with Dirichlet, Neumann, and `du/dt = ` boundary conditions, respectively.  These functions `f, g, h` all take the form `(x, y, t, u, p) -> Number`. Note that, with this setup, the boundary conditions structure carries along the mesh with it.
- In terms of derivative information, an integral formulation for the equations I deal with is

$$
\frac{\mathrm d}{\mathrm dt}\iint_{\Omega_i} u\,\mathrm dV +  \sum_{\sigma \in \mathcal E_i} \int_\sigma \boldsymbol q \cdot \boldsymbol{\hat n}_{i, \sigma}\,\mathrm ds = \iint_{\Omega_i} R\,\mathrm dV,
$$

where $\Omega\_i$ is the $i$th control volume. The only approximations I make from here are:

1. The line integrals are approximated by a midpoint rule, 

$$ 
\int_\sigma \boldsymbol q \cdot \hat{\boldsymbol n}_{i, \sigma}\,\mathrm ds \approx [\boldsymbol q(x_\sigma, y_\sigma, t, u) \cdot \boldsymbol{\hat n}_{i, \sigma}]L_\sigma;
$$

2. The integrals $\iint_{\Omega_i} u \mathrm dV$ and $\iint_{\Omega_i} R\,\mathrm dV$ are replaced by $V_i u_i$ and $V_i R_i$, respectively, where $u_i$ is the value of $u$ at the $i$th node (corresponding to the node that defines $\Omega_i$), and similarly for $R_i$.

3. The function $\boldsymbol q(x_\sigma, y_\sigma, t, u)$ also needs to be approximated, since this could contain e.g. gradients. For this purpose, a linear interpolant is used, replacing any instance of $u$ with $\alpha x + \beta y + \gamma$ (with $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ estimated over $\Omega_i$; see [here](https://danielvandh.github.io/FiniteVolumeMethod.jl/stable/math/)). With this setup, the actual interface I used for defining $\boldsymbol q$ is through functions of the form `flux(x, y, t, α, β, γ, p)`, so that any instance of `u` is replaced with `αx + βy + γ`, and e.g. gradients $\boldsymbol \nabla u(x, y, t)$ become $(\alpha, \beta)^T$.


So, the domain types are supposed to be any; the main equation just needs to be provided with a function `flux(x, y, t, α, β, γ, p)` for $\boldsymbol q$ and `R(x, y, t, u, p)` for the source term; the boundary conditions are provided with functions of the form `f(x, y, t, u, p)` together with a label for the boundary condition type, given in an order corresponding to the boundary order); line integrals are approximated with midpoint rules while volume integrals are replaced with the volume of the integral times the integrand's value at the center; $u$ is represented as a linear function across each linear interpolant.

How's that?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/PDEBase.jl/issues/comments/1491080422/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

> is necessary for any use of DifferentialEquations.jl or the packages that are maintained as part of its suite (OrdinaryDiffEq.jl, Sundials.jl, DiffEqDevTools.jl, etc.).

This is the start of a new sentence. I'm assumed this should be a continuation of "we would be grateful if you could cite our work." above. Perhaps the confusing sentence above is meant to be "as necessary for any use of...", and the period removed in "we would be grateful if you could cite our work" to make the continuation clearer.

I also think two capitals are missing in the BibTeX - should it be

>   title={Differential{E}quations.jl--a performant and feature-rich ecosystem for solving differential equations in {J}ulia},

section","user":{"login":"DanielVandH","id":95613936,"node_id":"U_kgDOBbLz8A","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/95613936?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH","html_url":"https://github.com/DanielVandH","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2023-02-09T03:50:05Z","updated_at":"2023-02-15T13:49:17Z","closed_at":"2023-02-15T13:49:17Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Maybe I'm just misreading this for some reason, but in the "Supporting and Citing" section of https://docs.sciml.ai/DiffEqDocs/stable/, there is a sentence after the BibTeX that reads

> is necessary for any use of DifferentialEquations.jl or the packages that are maintained as part of its suite (OrdinaryDiffEq.jl, Sundials.jl, DiffEqDevTools.jl, etc.).

This is the start of a new sentence. I'm assumed this should be a continuation of "we would be grateful if you could cite our work." above. Perhaps the confusing sentence above is meant to be "as necessary for any use of...", and the period removed in "we would be grateful if you could cite our work" to make the continuation clearer.

I also think two capitals are missing in the BibTeX - should it be

>   title={Differential{E}quations.jl--a performant and feature-rich ecosystem for solving differential equations in {J}ulia},

{"action":"opened","number":147,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Integrals.jl/pulls/147","id":1245289996,"node_id":"PR_kwDODABHdc5KOZ4M","html_url":"https://github.com/SciML/Integrals.jl/pull/147","diff_url":"https://github.com/SciML/Integrals.jl/pull/147.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/SciML/Integrals.jl/pull/147.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Integrals.jl/issues/147","number":147,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"Add Gauss-Legendre quadrature","user":{"login":"DanielVandH","id":95613936,"node_id":"U_kgDOBbLz8A","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/95613936?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH","html_url":"https://github.com/DanielVandH","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"This is an initial implementation of Gauss-Legendre quadrature, with support for composite quadrature, i.e. I implement both

$$
\int_a^b f(x)~\mathrm{dx} \approx \frac{b-a}{2}\sum_{k=1}^n A_kf\left(\frac{b-a}{2}x_k + \frac{b+a}{2}\right)
$$

and 

$$
\int_a^b f(x)~\mathrm{dx} \approx \frac{h}{2}\sum_{j=2}^m \sum_{k=1}^n A_k f\left(\frac{h}{2}\xi_k + \frac{x_{j-1} + x_j}{2}\right),
$$

where $A_k = 2/[(1-\xi_k^2)[P_n'(\xi_k)]^2]$ are the quadrature weights and $\xi_k$ are the roots of the $n$th degree Legendre polynomial $P_n$. In the latter case, $[a, b]$ is split into $m-1$ subintervals $[x_{j-1}, x_j]$, $j=2,\ldots,m$, each of equal width $h = (b-a)/(m-1)$ and Gauss-Legendre quadrature is applied on each interval separately.

Some other thoughts for more types of Gaussian quadrature are below, but the above is perhaps enough for now to get a feel for what it should look like.

I don't think it would be too difficult to make this more general for other types of Gauss Quadrature, defining an approach e.g. for evaluating 

$$
\int_a^b w(x) f(x)~\mathrm{dx} \approx \sum_{k=1}^n W_k f(x_k)
$$

with $W_k$ and $x_k$ the weights and nodes. Perhaps a type like

```julia
abstract type AbstractGaussianQuadrature{C,A,F,N,W} end
```

could be used, with `C` deciding if a composite rule should be used, `A` deciding if the weight function has already been applied onto $f$ (if not, we need to define $g(x) = f(x)/w(x)$ so that we have an integrand of the form $w(x) g(x)$), and `N` and `W` are for the nodes and weights, respectively. Maybe the weight function could be stored directly in `F`. With this, for example, my `GaussLegendre` struct could build a `GaussianQuadrature` type directly - the actual code for applying the rule is the same in all cases. Could obviously keep going with e.g. Newton-Cotes rules and error estimates but that's not for this PR..

quadrature","user":{"login":"DanielVandH","id":95613936,"node_id":"U_kgDOBbLz8A","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/95613936?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH","html_url":"https://github.com/DanielVandH","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"This is an initial implementation of Gauss-Legendre quadrature, with support for composite quadrature, i.e. I implement both

$$
\int_a^b f(x)~\mathrm{dx} \approx \frac{b-a}{2}\sum_{k=1}^n A_kf\left(\frac{b-a}{2}x_k + \frac{b+a}{2}\right)
$$

and 

$$
\int_a^b f(x)~\mathrm{dx} \approx \frac{h}{2}\sum_{j=2}^m \sum_{k=1}^n A_k f\left(\frac{h}{2}\xi_k + \frac{x_{j-1} + x_j}{2}\right),
$$

where $A_k = 2/[(1-\xi_k^2)[P_n'(\xi_k)]^2]$ are the quadrature weights and $\xi_k$ are the roots of the $n$th degree Legendre polynomial $P_n$. In the latter case, $[a, b]$ is split into $m-1$ subintervals $[x_{j-1}, x_j]$, $j=2,\ldots,m$, each of equal width $h = (b-a)/(m-1)$ and Gauss-Legendre quadrature is applied on each interval separately.

Some other thoughts for more types of Gaussian quadrature are below, but the above is perhaps enough for now to get a feel for what it should look like.

I don't think it would be too difficult to make this more general for other types of Gauss Quadrature, defining an approach e.g. for evaluating 

$$
\int_a^b w(x) f(x)~\mathrm{dx} \approx \sum_{k=1}^n W_k f(x_k)
$$

with $W_k$ and $x_k$ the weights and nodes. Perhaps a type like

```julia
abstract type AbstractGaussianQuadrature{C,A,F,N,W} end
```

could be used, with `C` deciding if a composite rule should be used, `A` deciding if the weight function has already been applied onto $f$ (if not, we need to define $g(x) = f(x)/w(x)$ so that we have an integrand of the form $w(x) g(x)$), and `N` and `W` are for the nodes and weights, respectively. Maybe the weight function could be stored directly in `F`. With this, for example, my `GaussLegendre` struct could build a `GaussianQuadrature` type directly - the actual code for applying the rule is the same in all cases. Could obviously keep going with e.g. Newton-Cotes rules and error estimates but that's not for this PR..

An important issue to consider would be avoiding mapping away from infinity if the quadrature rule calls for it, or mapping into infinity if needed (e.g. Gauss-Hermite or Gauss-Laguerre)

Gauss-Legendre quadrature","user":{"login":"DanielVandH","id":95613936,"node_id":"U_kgDOBbLz8A","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/95613936?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH","html_url":"https://github.com/DanielVandH","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"This is an initial implementation of Gauss-Legendre quadrature, with support for composite quadrature, i.e. I implement both

$$
\int_a^b f(x)~\mathrm{dx} \approx \frac{b-a}{2}\sum_{k=1}^n A_kf\left(\frac{b-a}{2}x_k + \frac{b+a}{2}\right)
$$

and 

$$
\int_a^b f(x)~\mathrm{dx} \approx \frac{h}{2}\sum_{j=2}^m \sum_{k=1}^n A_k f\left(\frac{h}{2}\xi_k + \frac{x_{j-1} + x_j}{2}\right),
$$

where $A_k = 2/[(1-\xi_k^2)[P_n'(\xi_k)]^2]$ are the quadrature weights and $\xi_k$ are the roots of the $n$th degree Legendre polynomial $P_n$. In the latter case, $[a, b]$ is split into $m-1$ subintervals $[x_{j-1}, x_j]$, $j=2,\ldots,m$, each of equal width $h = (b-a)/(m-1)$ and Gauss-Legendre quadrature is applied on each interval separately.

Some other thoughts for more types of Gaussian quadrature are below, but the above is perhaps enough for now to get a feel for what it should look like.

I don't think it would be too difficult to make this more general for other types of Gauss Quadrature, defining an approach e.g. for evaluating 

$$
\int_a^b w(x) f(x)~\mathrm{dx} \approx \sum_{k=1}^n W_k f(x_k)
$$

with $W_k$ and $x_k$ the weights and nodes. Perhaps a type like

```julia
abstract type AbstractGaussianQuadrature{C,A,F,N,W} end
```

could be used, with `C` deciding if a composite rule should be used, `A` deciding if the weight function has already been applied onto $f$ (if not, we need to define $g(x) = f(x)/w(x)$ so that we have an integrand of the form $w(x) g(x)$), and `N` and `W` are for the nodes and weights, respectively. Maybe the weight function could be stored directly in `F`. With this, for example, my `GaussLegendre` struct could build a `GaussianQuadrature` type directly - the actual code for applying the rule is the same in all cases. Could obviously keep going with e.g. Newton-Cotes rules and error estimates but that's not for this PR..

An important issue to consider would be avoiding mapping away from infinity if the quadrature rule calls for it, or mapping into infinity if needed (e.g. Gauss-Hermite or Gauss-Laguerre)

+module IntegralsFastGaussQuadratureExt
+using Integrals
+if isdefined(Base, :get_extension)
+    import FastGaussQuadrature
+    import FastGaussQuadrature: gausslegendre
+    # and eventually gausschebyshev, etc.
+else
+    import ..FastGaussQuadrature
+    import ..FastGaussQuadrature: gausslegendre
+end
+using LinearAlgebra
+
+Integrals.gausslegendre(n) = FastGaussQuadrature.gausslegendre(n)
+
+function gauss_legendre(f, p, lb, ub, nodes, weights)
+    scale = (ub - lb) / 2
+    shift = (lb + ub) / 2
+    scaled_f = s -> f(scale * s + shift, quadrature","user":{"login":"DanielVandH","id":95613936,"node_id":"U_kgDOBbLz8A","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/95613936?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH","html_url":"https://github.com/DanielVandH","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"This is an initial implementation of Gauss-Legendre quadrature, with support for composite quadrature, i.e. I implement both

$$
\int_a^b f(x)~\mathrm{dx} \approx \frac{b-a}{2}\sum_{k=1}^n A_kf\left(\frac{b-a}{2}x_k + \frac{b+a}{2}\right)
$$

and 

$$
\int_a^b f(x)~\mathrm{dx} \approx \frac{h}{2}\sum_{j=2}^m \sum_{k=1}^n A_k f\left(\frac{h}{2}\xi_k + \frac{x_{j-1} + x_j}{2}\right),
$$

where $A_k = 2/[(1-\xi_k^2)[P_n'(\xi_k)]^2]$ are the quadrature weights and $\xi_k$ are the roots of the $n$th degree Legendre polynomial $P_n$. In the latter case, $[a, b]$ is split into $m-1$ subintervals $[x_{j-1}, x_j]$, $j=2,\ldots,m$, each of equal width $h = (b-a)/(m-1)$ and Gauss-Legendre quadrature is applied on each interval separately.

Some other thoughts for more types of Gaussian quadrature are below, but the above is perhaps enough for now to get a feel for what it should look like.

I don't think it would be too difficult to make this more general for other types of Gauss Quadrature, defining an approach e.g. for evaluating 

$$
\int_a^b w(x) f(x)~\mathrm{dx} \approx \sum_{k=1}^n W_k f(x_k)
$$

with $W_k$ and $x_k$ the weights and nodes. Perhaps a type like

```julia
abstract type AbstractGaussianQuadrature{C,A,F,N,W} end
```

could be used, with `C` deciding if a composite rule should be used, `A` deciding if the weight function has already been applied onto $f$ (if not, we need to define $g(x) = f(x)/w(x)$ so that we have an integrand of the form $w(x) g(x)$), and `N` and `W` are for the nodes and weights, respectively. Maybe the weight function could be stored directly in `F`. With this, for example, my `GaussLegendre` struct could build a `GaussianQuadrature` type directly - the actual code for applying the rule is the same in all cases. Could obviously keep going with e.g. Newton-Cotes rules and error estimates but that's not for this PR..

An important issue to consider would be avoiding mapping away from infinity if the quadrature rule calls for it, or mapping into infinity if needed (e.g. Gauss-Hermite or Gauss-Laguerre)

Gauss-Legendre quadrature","user":{"login":"DanielVandH","id":95613936,"node_id":"U_kgDOBbLz8A","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/95613936?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH","html_url":"https://github.com/DanielVandH","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"This is an initial implementation of Gauss-Legendre quadrature, with support for composite quadrature, i.e. I implement both

$$
\int_a^b f(x)~\mathrm{dx} \approx \frac{b-a}{2}\sum_{k=1}^n A_kf\left(\frac{b-a}{2}x_k + \frac{b+a}{2}\right)
$$

and 

$$
\int_a^b f(x)~\mathrm{dx} \approx \frac{h}{2}\sum_{j=2}^m \sum_{k=1}^n A_k f\left(\frac{h}{2}\xi_k + \frac{x_{j-1} + x_j}{2}\right),
$$

where $A_k = 2/[(1-\xi_k^2)[P_n'(\xi_k)]^2]$ are the quadrature weights and $\xi_k$ are the roots of the $n$th degree Legendre polynomial $P_n$. In the latter case, $[a, b]$ is split into $m-1$ subintervals $[x_{j-1}, x_j]$, $j=2,\ldots,m$, each of equal width $h = (b-a)/(m-1)$ and Gauss-Legendre quadrature is applied on each interval separately.

Some other thoughts for more types of Gaussian quadrature are below, but the above is perhaps enough for now to get a feel for what it should look like.

I don't think it would be too difficult to make this more general for other types of Gauss Quadrature, defining an approach e.g. for evaluating 

$$
\int_a^b w(x) f(x)~\mathrm{dx} \approx \sum_{k=1}^n W_k f(x_k)
$$

with $W_k$ and $x_k$ the weights and nodes. Perhaps a type like

```julia
abstract type AbstractGaussianQuadrature{C,A,F,N,W} end
```

could be used, with `C` deciding if a composite rule should be used, `A` deciding if the weight function has already been applied onto $f$ (if not, we need to define $g(x) = f(x)/w(x)$ so that we have an integrand of the form $w(x) g(x)$), and `N` and `W` are for the nodes and weights, respectively. Maybe the weight function could be stored directly in `F`. With this, for example, my `GaussLegendre` struct could build a `GaussianQuadrature` type directly - the actual code for applying the rule is the same in all cases. Could obviously keep going with e.g. Newton-Cotes rules and error estimates but that's not for this PR..

An important issue to consider would be avoiding mapping away from infinity if the quadrature rule calls for it, or mapping into infinity if needed (e.g. Gauss-Hermite or Gauss-Laguerre)

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Integrals.jl/issues/149","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Integrals.jl","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Integrals.jl/issues/149/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Integrals.jl/issues/149/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Integrals.jl/issues/149/events","html_url":"https://github.com/SciML/Integrals.jl/issues/149","id":1590141599,"node_id":"I_kwDODABHdc5ex6Kf","number":149,"title":"More types of infinity transformations","user":{"login":"DanielVandH","id":95613936,"node_id":"U_kgDOBbLz8A","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/95613936?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH","html_url":"https://github.com/DanielVandH","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2023-02-18T00:58:45Z","updated_at":"2023-02-18T00:58:45Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Currently the only type of transformation used for handling infinite bounds is $u \mapsto a + t/(1-t)$ and $u \mapsto t/(1-t)^2$. It could be nice to eventually support other transformations.

In some of the integrals I've dealt with in my work, I've found that transformations like 

$$
s = -\cot\left[\frac{\left(\pi + 2\arctan(a)\right)(\xi+1)}{4}\right], \quad -1 < \xi < 1,
$$

and 

$$
s = -\cot\left[\frac{\left(\pi - 2\arctan(a)\right)(s-1)}{4}\right], \quad -1 < \xi < 1,
$$

could be useful, giving the (rather complicated..) results

$$
\int_{-\infty}^a g(s)~\mathrm{d}s = \frac{1}{4}\left(\pi + 2\arctan(a)\right)\int_{-1}^1 \csc^2\left[\frac{1}{4}\left(s+1\right)\left(2\arctan(a) + \pi\right)\right]g\left(-\cot\left[\frac{\left(\pi + 2\arctan(a)\right)(s+1)}{4}\right]\right)~\mathrm{d}s,
$$

$$
\int_a^\infty g(s)~\mathrm{d}s = \frac{1}{4}\left(\pi - 2\arctan(a)\right)\int_{-1}^1 \csc^2\left[\frac{1}{4}\left(s-1\right)\left(\pi - 2\arctan(a)\right)\right]g\left(-\cot\left[\frac{\left(\pi - 2\arctan(a)\right)\left(s-1\right)}{4}\right]\right)~\mathrm{d}s
$$

which typically gave better results when applying Gauss-Legendre quadrature afterwards. These integrals I dealt with had issues with oscillations and singularities, etc., so the currently used transform is still a good default. Another useful transform is $t \mapsto (2/\pi)\arctan(t)$, giving 

$$
\int_{-\infty}^\infty g(t)~\mathrm{d}t = \frac{\pi}{2}\int_{-1}^1 \sec^2\left(\frac{\pi t}{2}\right)g\left(\tan\frac{\pi t}{2}\right)~\mathrm{d}t.
$$

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/TruncatedStacktraces.jl/issues/2","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/TruncatedStacktraces.jl","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/TruncatedStacktraces.jl/issues/2/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/TruncatedStacktraces.jl/issues/2/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/TruncatedStacktraces.jl/issues/2/events","html_url":"https://github.com/SciML/TruncatedStacktraces.jl/issues/2","id":1599866216,"node_id":"I_kwDOJCGpa85fXAVo","number":2,"title":"VERBOSE does not change anything","user":{"login":"DanielVandH","id":95613936,"node_id":"U_kgDOBbLz8A","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/95613936?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH","html_url":"https://github.com/DanielVandH","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2023-02-25T22:56:41Z","updated_at":"2023-02-25T22:56:41Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"```julia
julia> using TruncatedStacktraces;

julia> TruncatedStacktraces.VERBOSE[] = false;

julia> v
ERROR: UndefVarError: v not defined

Some of the types have been truncated in the stacktrace for improved reading. To emit complete information
in the stack trace, evaluate `TruncatedStacktraces.VERBOSE[] = true` and re-run the code.


julia> TruncatedStacktraces.VERBOSE[] = true;

julia> v
ERROR: UndefVarError: v not defined

Some of the types have been truncated in the stacktrace for improved reading. To emit complete information
in the stack trace, evaluate `TruncatedStacktraces.VERBOSE[] = true` and re-run the code.
```

Doesn't look like VERBOSE does anything here - I also question whether this error message should be printed when no types are even present in the error message. Perhaps the `init` function in the src file needs to be something like

```julia
function __init__()
    for type in InteractiveUtils.subtypes(Exception)
        if type == MethodError
            Base.Experimental.register_error_hint(type) do io, e, args, kwargs
                VERBOSE[] && println(io, VERBOSE_MSG)
            end
        else
            Base.Experimental.register_error_hint(type) do io, e
                VERBOSE[] && println(io, VERBOSE_MSG)
            end
        end
    end
end
```

or maybe `VERBOSE[]` goes before `Base.Experimental.register_error_hint(type)` - I'm not really sure what `register_error_hint` does.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/TruncatedStacktraces.jl/issues/2/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/TruncatedStacktraces.jl/issues/2/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/TruncatedStacktraces.jl/issues/2","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/TruncatedStacktraces.jl","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/TruncatedStacktraces.jl/issues/2/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/TruncatedStacktraces.jl/issues/2/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/TruncatedStacktraces.jl/issues/2/events","html_url":"https://github.com/SciML/TruncatedStacktraces.jl/issues/2","id":1599866216,"node_id":"I_kwDOJCGpa85fXAVo","number":2,"title":"VERBOSE does not change anything","user":{"login":"DanielVandH","id":95613936,"node_id":"U_kgDOBbLz8A","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/95613936?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH","html_url":"https://github.com/DanielVandH","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2023-02-25T22:56:41Z","updated_at":"2023-02-26T05:12:02Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"```julia
julia> using TruncatedStacktraces;

julia> TruncatedStacktraces.VERBOSE[] = false;

julia> v
ERROR: UndefVarError: v not defined

Some of the types have been truncated in the stacktrace for improved reading. To emit complete information
in the stack trace, evaluate `TruncatedStacktraces.VERBOSE[] = true` and re-run the code.


julia> TruncatedStacktraces.VERBOSE[] = true;

julia> v
ERROR: UndefVarError: v not defined

Some of the types have been truncated in the stacktrace for improved reading. To emit complete information
in the stack trace, evaluate `TruncatedStacktraces.VERBOSE[] = true` and re-run the code.
```

Doesn't look like VERBOSE does anything here - I also question whether this error message should be printed when no types are even present in the error message. Perhaps the `init` function in the src file needs to be something like

```julia
function __init__()
    for type in InteractiveUtils.subtypes(Exception)
        if type == MethodError
            Base.Experimental.register_error_hint(type) do io, e, args, kwargs
                VERBOSE[] && println(io, VERBOSE_MSG)
            end
        else
            Base.Experimental.register_error_hint(type) do io, e
                VERBOSE[] && println(io, VERBOSE_MSG)
            end
        end
    end
end
```

 du[2] = u[1]*(28.0-u[3]) - u[2]
 du[3] = u[1]*u[2] - (8/3)*u[3]
end
u0 = [1.0;0.0;0.0]
tspan = (0.0,100.0)
prob = ODEProblem(lorenz!,u0,tspan) |> NonlinearProblem
alg = NewtonRaphson(linsolve=KrylovJL_GMRES())
sol = solve(prob, alg)
```

```julia
using Optimization
using NLopt
rosenbrock(x, p) =  (p[1] - x[1])^2 + p[2] * (x[2] - x[1]^2)^2
x0 = zeros(2)
p  = [1.0, 100.0]
f = OptimizationFunction(rosenbrock)
prob = Optimization.OptimizationProblem(f, x0, p, lb = [-1.0,-1.0], ub = [1.0,1.0])
sol = solve(prob, NLopt.LN_NELDERMEAD())
ERROR: MethodError: objects of type Algorithm are not callable
Stacktrace:
 [1] top-level scope
   @ Untitled-1:8
```
this is fixed when doing `using OptimizationNLopt` (and matches the rest of the {"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Optimization.jl/issues/455","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Optimization.jl","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Optimization.jl/issues/455/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Optimization.jl/issues/455/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Optimization.jl/issues/455/events","html_url":"https://github.com/SciML/Optimization.jl/issues/455","id":1529734640,"node_id":"I_kwDOD5kVYs5bLeXw","number":455,"title":"ReturnCodes from NLopt are not properly converted","user":{"login":"DanielVandH","id":95613936,"node_id":"U_kgDOBbLz8A","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/95613936?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH","html_url":"https://github.com/DanielVandH","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2023-01-11T21:38:49Z","updated_at":"2023-01-11T21:38:49Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Identified from https://github.com/DanielVandH/ProfileLikelihood.jl/issues/81, successful results from NLopt are not properly converted, and instead just report failures. 

With Optimization.jl:
```julia
using Optimization
using OptimizationNLopt
rosenbrock(x, p) =  (p[1] - x[1])^2 + p[2] * (x[2] - x[1]^2)^2
x0 = zeros(2)
p  = [1.0, 100.0]
f = OptimizationFunction(rosenbrock)
prob = Optimization.OptimizationProblem(f, x0, p, lb = [-1.0,-1.0], ub = [1.0,1.0])
sol = solve(prob, NLopt.LN_NELDERMEAD)
sol.retcode
```
```julia
ReturnCode.Failure = 9
```

With NLopt.jl directly:
```julia
using NLopt
loss_fnc = (x, g) -> rosenbrock(x, p)
alg = Opt(:LN_NELDERMEAD, 2)
alg.min_objective = loss_fnc
alg.lower_bounds = [-1.0,-1.0]
alg.upper_bounds = [1.0, 1.0]
NLopt.optimize(alg, x0)[3]
```
```julia
:XTOL_REACHED
```

I think the problem is here https://github.com/SciML/SciMLBase.jl/blob/e5af2fd2ff1c992fdb1ee8e9d71e00e593ded6c5/src/retcodes.jl#L390-L420, which is missing the symbols from NLopt, which I think are (https://nlopt.readthedocs.io/en/latest/NLopt_Reference/#successful-termination-positive-return-values)

```julia
:SUCCESS
:STOPVAL_REACHED 
:FTOL_REACHED 
:XTOL_REACHED
:MAXEVAL_REACHED
MAXTIME_REACHED
:FAILURE
:INVALID_ARGS 
:OUT_OF_MEMORY
:ROUNDOFF_LIMITED 
:FORCED_STOP
```

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/DiffEqCallbacks.jl/issues/133","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/DiffEqCallbacks.jl","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/DiffEqCallbacks.jl/issues/133/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/DiffEqCallbacks.jl/issues/133/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/DiffEqCallbacks.jl/issues/133/events","html_url":"https://github.com/SciML/DiffEqCallbacks.jl/issues/133","id":1533537962,"node_id":"I_kwDOBKkOHs5bZ-6q","number":133,"title":"GeneralDomain documentation is unclear","user":{"login":"DanielVandH","id":95613936,"node_id":"U_kgDOBbLz8A","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/95613936?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH","html_url":"https://github.com/DanielVandH","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2023-01-14T22:46:46Z","updated_at":"2023-01-14T22:46:46Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"The documentation for `GeneralDomain` shows the docstring

```
GeneralDomain(g, u=nothing; nlsolve=NLSOLVEJL_SETUP(), save=true,
                       abstol=nothing, scalefactor=nothing, autonomous=numargs(g)==2,
                       nlopts=Dict(:ftol => 10*eps()))
```

which should now be

```
GeneralDomain(g, u = nothing; nlsolve = NLSOLVEJL_SETUP(), save = true,
                       abstol = nothing, scalefactor = nothing,
                       autonomous = maximum(SciMLBase.numargs(g)) == 3,
                       nlopts = Dict(:ftol => 10 * eps()))
```

Moreover, it initially says that `g` should be of the form `g(u, resid)` or `g(t, u, resid)`, and then later says it should be `g(resid, u, p)` or `g(resid, u, p, t)`. What should it be? It's not exactly clear to me what the "residuals" are either: a vector that tests each component of the solution? A single value testing all components? I thought it would be the former, but the documentation says 

```
- `g`: ... which is zero when the value is in the domain.
```

which suggests the latter.

The default should be `min_sense` and you might even want to only implement that in the first iteration, but you want to do the API for this correctly from the beginning.

certainly `MIN_SENSE` and `MAX_SENSE` are necessary.  The last one there is `FEASIBILITY_SENSE` is for when you are using an optimizer to solve a feasibility problem, but not sure if it is only used in problem transformation or not.  The feasibility approach is to solve a system of equations by using the nonlinear optimizer/etc. to find a feasible point for the constraints where you don't care about the objective value.

Regardless, it means that you wouldn't want to design as a `min vs max` toggle in case `feasibility` or others should come The default should be `min_sense` and you might even want to only implement that in the first iteration, but you want to do the API for this correctly from the beginning.

certainly `MIN_SENSE` and `MAX_SENSE` are necessary.  The last one there is `FEASIBILITY_SENSE` is for when you are using an optimizer to solve a feasibility problem, but not sure if it is only used in problem transformation or not.  The feasibility approach is to solve a system of equations by using the nonlinear optimizer/etc. to find a feasible point for the constraints where you don't care about the objective value.

Regardless, it means that you wouldn't want to design as a `min vs max` toggle in case `feasibility` or others should come later.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Optimization.jl/issues/8/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Optimization.jl/issues/8/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Optimization.jl/issues/comments/1340608603","html_url":"https://github.com/SciML/Optimization.jl/issues/8#issuecomment-1340608603","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/Optimization.jl/issues/8","id":1340608603,"node_id":"IC_kwDOD5kVYs5P6BBb","user":{"login":"DanielVandH","id":95613936,"node_id":"U_kgDOBbLz8A","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/95613936?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH","html_url":"https://github.com/DanielVandH","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/DanielVandH/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2022-12-07T08:57:48Z","updated_at":"2022-12-07T08:57:48Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"@Vaibhavdixit02 Done. it be surprising that the results when using this cache are not inferrable? e.g.

```julia
using Random, OrdinaryDiffEq, LinearAlgebra, Optimization, OptimizationOptimJL,
    PreallocationTools

function testprob()
    lbc = GeneralLazyBufferCache(function (p)
        SciMLBase.init(ODEProblem(ode_fnc, y₀,
                (0.0, T), p),
            Tsit5(); saveat=t)
    end)

    Random.seed!(2992999)
    λ, y₀, σ = -0.5, 15.0, 0.1
    T, n = 5.0, 200
    Δt = T / n
    t = [j * Δt for j in 0:n]
    y = y₀ * exp.(λ * t)
    yᵒ = y .+ [0.0, σ * randn(n)...]
    ode_fnc(u, p, t) = p * u
    function loglik(θ, data, integrator)
        yᵒ, n, ε = data
        λ, σ, u0 = θ
        integrator.p = λ
        reinit!(integrator, u0)
        solve!(integrator)
        ε = yᵒ .- integrator.sol.u
        ℓ = -0.5n * log(2π * σ^2) - 0.5 / σ^2 * sum(ε .^ 2)
    end
    θ₀ = [-1.0, 0.5, 19.73]
    negloglik = (θ, p) -> -loglik(θ, p, lbc[θ[1]])
    fnc = OptimizationFunction(negloglik, Optimization.AutoForwardDiff())
    ε = zeros(n)
    prob = OptimizationProblem(fnc, θ₀, (yᵒ, n, ε), lb=[-10.0, 1e-6, 0.5],
        ub=[10.0, 10.0, 25.0])
    return prob
end 

prob = testprob()

@code_warntype prob.f([-1.0,0.5,19.73], prob.p)
```

In doing so I am having some difficulties dealing with iteration and indexing, and the error messages don't really seem so clear to me in how I can fix it.

The following code covers the type of iteration I am dealing with:
```
using DifferentialEquations, ModelingToolkit, Symbolics
@inline do_something(k) = sin(k^2)
@register_symbolic do_something(k) 
@inline lookup_val(dict, idx) = dict[idx] # getindex
@register_symbolic lookup_val(dict, idx)
@inline set_val!(dict, val, idx) = dict[idx] = val # setindex!
@register_symbolic set_val!(dict, val, idx)
function ode_f_sym(du, u, p, t)
    vals, u_idx, itr_idx = p 
    for (i, j) in itr_idx
        set_val!(du, lookup_val(vals, i) * lookup_val(u, j), i)
    end
    for k in u_idx 
        set_val!(du, lookup_val(du, k) + do_something(lookup_val(u, k)), k)
    end
    nothing 
end 
a, b, c, d, e = 1.7, 2.3, -2.0, 10.0, 7.19
u_idx = [2, 3, 5, 4, 1] 
itr_idx = Dict(1 => 3, 2 => 5, 3 => 1, 4 => 4, 5 => 2)
prob = ODEProblem(ode_f_sym, [0.0, 1.0, 2.3, -2.0, 1.0], (0.0, 1.0), ((a, b, c, d, e), u_idx, itr_idx))
modelingtoolkitize(prob)
```
I define additional functions for `getindex` and `setindex!` so that they are not traced (as someone helped me with here https://discourse.julialang.org/t/modelingtoolkit-indexing-a-parameter-by-another-parameter-in-an-odeproblem/83569/2?u=legola18), along with some additional `do_something` function. When I run `modelingtoolkitize` as shown, I get:
```
ERROR: BoundsError: attempt to access Num at index [2]
Stacktrace:
 [1] indexed_iterate(I::Num, i::Int64, state::Nothing)
   @ Base .\tuple.jl:98

   @ Main c:\Users\licer\.julia\dev\FVM\dev\sparsity.jl:104
 [3] (::ODEFunction{true, typeof(ode_f_sym), LinearAlgebra.UniformScaling{Bool}, Nothing, Nothing, Nothing, Nothing, Nothing, Nothing, Nothing, Nothing, Nothing, Nothing, Nothing, Nothing, typeof(SciMLBase.DEFAULT_OBSERVED), Nothing})(::Vector{Num}, ::Vararg{Any})

```
How can I change the code further to allow for this type of work? It would be nice if there were e.g. a suggestion in this error message for what could be done for this type of loop, too - I know improving these error messages is a current work in progress.

Just for reference, this is what the code above looks like with hard-coded numbers (to make it easier to compare to for what 'works') and the Jacobian that I actually want to compute:
```
@inline do_something(k) = sin(k^2)
function ode_f_basic(du, u, p, t)
    vals= p 
    du[1] = vals[1] * u[3] 
    du[2] = vals[2] * u[5] 
    du[3] = vals[3] * u[1] 
    du[4] = vals[4] * u[4] 
    du[5] = vals[5] * u[2]
    du[2] += do_something(u[2])
    du[3] += do_something(u[3])
    du[5] += do_something(u[5])
    nothing 
end 
a, b, c, d, e = 1.7, 2.3, -2.0, 10.0, 7.19
u_idx = [2, 3, 5] 
itr_idx = Dict(1 => 3, 2 => 5, 3 => 1, 4 => 4, 5 => 2)
prob = ODEProblem(ode_f_basic, [0.0, 1.0, 2.3, -2.0, 1.0], (0.0, 1.0), ((a, b, c, d, e)))
de = modelingtoolkitize(prob)
calculate_jacobian(de)
```

the documentation of http://optimization.sciml.ai/stable/optimization_packages/multistartoptimization/ (and maybe other places, haven't looked beyond here), the "s" in "start" should not be capitalised.

Found this by noting that the instructions
```
(test) pkg> add OptimizationMultiStartOptimization
ERROR: The following package names could not be resolved:
 * OptimizationMultiStartOptimization (not found in project, manifest or registry)
   Suggestions: OptimizationMultistartOptimization
```
doesn't work, because it should be
```
(test) pkg> add OptimizationMultistartOptimization
   Resolving package versions...
   Installed OptimizationMultistartOptimization ─ v0.1.0
```
